extracted_claims: It simulates the real network workload — segments arriving in real time, multiple concurrent sessions — and tells you exactly how many streams your GPU can handle without falling behind.

It: Takes an HLS stream and a set of output rendition profiles as input Simulates live mode by default — queuing segments one by one as they arrive, just as a real gateway would send them Runs multiple concurrent transcoding sessions (configurable) Outputs per segment metrics as CSV and a summary table The key output metric is the Real Time Duration Ratio: total transcoding time divided by total source duration.
A ratio below 1.0 means you're transcoding faster than real time.
A ratio above 1.0 means your GPU is falling behind.

Download and extract the provided 1 minute test stream: Download the standard output rendition configuration: This file defines the four standard output renditions: Profile Resolution Bitrate 426×240 250 kbps 640×360 800 kbps 854×480 1,600 kbps 1,280×720 3,000 kbps Running the benchmark Basic run Flag reference: Flag Description Default Path to the source manifest Required Path to JSON rendition config, or comma separated profile string Comma separated GPU IDs for NVIDIA hardware transcoding Omit for CPU Number of simultaneous transcoding sessions Simulate live stream (queue segments one by one) Omit if you are testing CPU transcoding.
Live mode vs VOD mode By default, queues segments as they would arrive in a live stream — one every 2 seconds.

This is the primary capacity metric: Ratio Meaning < 1.0 Transcoding faster than real time — GPU has headroom = 1.0 Exactly real time — no headroom, any spike will cause lag > 1.0 Slower than real time — GPU is overloaded at this session count Production threshold: A ratio ≤ 0.8 leaves approximately 20% headroom for upload/download overhead and transient load spikes.
The last concurrent session count at which you achieve ≤ 0.8 is your hardware session limit.
Session scaling test Run the benchmark across increasing concurrent session counts to find your hardware ceiling: This appends one line per session count to .
The output looks like: In this example, the hardware session limit is 4 — the last count where ratio ≤ 0.8.
If the ratio is still below 1.0 at 20 sessions, increase the range — replace with or higher.
an RTX 4090 and an A4000 in the same machine): benchmark each GPU separately by passing its individual ID to .

Understanding both matters before you begin: Revenue stream How it works Depends on ETH job fees Gateways pay you per transcoding segment or AI inference job Your capability, pricing, performance, uptime LPT inflation rewards Protocol mints LPT each round; you claim your share by calling Your total bonded stake, inflation rate, reward call reliability Neither stream pays from day one without setup.
ETH fees require gateways to route work to you.
LPT rewards require bonded LPT, an active orchestrator registration, and a running node that calls each round.
Consumer RTX cards (3060, 3070, 3080) are common.
AI inference (batch): 8–24 GB VRAM depending on which pipelines you want to run.
An RTX 3090 (24 GB) is the practical minimum for competitive diffusion pipelines.
AI inference (real time / Cascade): 24 GB VRAM minimum.
Real time pipelines are more demanding than batch.

Hardware you're buying to run Livepeer has an amortisation cost you should model explicitly: if you spend £800 on an RTX 4090 and earn £30/month, the payback period is over 2 years before electricity.
To receive video transcoding jobs as a solo orchestrator, you must be in the active set — currently the top orchestrators by total bonded stake.
LPT must be acquired and bonded to your orchestrator address.
Acquiring meaningful LPT at current market prices is the primary entry barrier for solo video orchestrators.
This page is a practical reference: GPU support policy, session limits by card, VRAM tiers by pipeline, and the minimum system stack needed before your node goes live.
GPU vendor support NVIDIA is the supported GPU vendor for go livepeer.
AMD and Intel GPUs are not supported for hardware accelerated transcoding or AI inference.
NVIDIA CUDA is required for both NVENC video transcoding and all AI inference pipelines.
Minimum CUDA version: 12.0.
Earlier versions are not supported by the AI runner containers.
NVENC/NVDEC session limits NVIDIA consumer GPUs impose artificial concurrent encoding session limits via NVENC (hardware video encoder) and NVDEC (hardware video decoder).
Consumer NVIDIA cards (GeForce RTX series) are limited to a small number of concurrent NVENC sessions by default.
Consumer cards are typically limited to 2–3 concurrent NVENC encode sessions before they throttle or fail with errors.

Key patterns: GPU tier NVENC session cap Notes GeForce RTX (consumer) 2–3 concurrent sessions Hardware licensing cap RTX Workstation / Quadro Uncapped Commercial hardware NVIDIA A series (A100, A4000, A5000) Uncapped Data centre NVIDIA L series (L4, L40) Uncapped Data centre NVIDIA H series (H100) Uncapped Data centre Exact session counts can vary by GPU generation and NVIDIA policy changes, so verify your specific card in the matrix rather than assuming a tier wide cap.

If you're running a consumer card, run the benchmark to find your practical transcoding capacity – the NVENC cap means your hardware limit from benchmarking will be low (2–3 sessions) rather than the higher number you might calculate from GPU compute alone.
It does not require large VRAM – model loading is not involved.
Your session limit is the ceiling on how many concurrent transcoding streams your node will accept.
Why session limits matter Go livepeer has a default maximum of 10 concurrent sessions ( ).
Bandwidth per stream Every transcoding job requires both download (receiving the source stream) and upload (delivering output renditions).

The current standard output rendition set in is: Profile Resolution Output bitrate 426×240 250 kbps 640×360 800 kbps 854×480 1,600 kbps 1,280×720 3,000 kbps Total output 5,650 kbps Upload bandwidth per stream: approximately 5.7 Mbps (sum of all output rendition bitrates).
For a 720p source at 3,000 kbps, download is approximately 3 Mbps per stream.
For a 1080p source, download could be 6 Mbps per stream or more.
For planning purposes, budget ~6 Mbps symmetric per concurrent stream to cover both source download and output upload with margin.

Estimating your stream count from bandwidth Examples: Available bandwidth Bandwidth limit (streams) With ~20% burst margin 100 Mbps symmetric ~16 streams ~13 streams 500 Mbps symmetric ~83 streams ~66 streams 1 Gbps symmetric ~166 streams ~133 streams The 20% burst margin accounts for segments arriving in bursts rather than perfectly spaced.
Many residential connections with 100 Mbps download have only 20–30 Mbps upload — the upload constraint dominates.

Deriving your session limit Your session limit is the minimum of your hardware limit and your bandwidth limit: Hardware limit — the last concurrent session count in your benchmark where the Real Time Duration Ratio was ≤ 0.8.

Example: Input Value Hardware limit (from benchmarking) 12 sessions Available bandwidth 100 Mbps symmetric Bandwidth limit (100 Mbps ÷ 6 Mbps, with 20% margin) ~13 sessions Session limit min(12, 13) = 12 In this example, hardware is the binding constraint.
The orchestrator uses it to know its total capacity; the transcoder uses it to control how many concurrent jobs it accepts.

verified_claims.summary: Split-setup guidance depends on OrchestratorSiphon still being an active community-maintained project. That claim should be checked against the current GitHub repository, not just repeated in docs.

snippets: Solo orchestrator / split setup / fleet: Yes.
Separate keystore management and reward calling (Siphon, secure machine) from workload processing (go livepeer, GPU machine).

Decision table Pool Worker Solo Orchestrator Split Setup (Siphon) Fleet LPT required No Yes Yes Yes (significant) GPU required Yes Yes Yes (GPU machine) Yes (multiple) On chain ops Pool handles it You manage Siphon handles it Dedicated ops Key security Pool's keystore Same machine as GPU Keystore isolated Dedicated secure machine Reward safety Pool handles it Risk if node is busy Siphon runs independently Dedicated reward infra Setup complexity Low Medium Medium High AI inference ready Depends on pool Yes, with aiModels.json Yes Yes Start here → → → → Contact us Workload type — decide before hardware or pricing Your path choice intersects with your workload choice.
Path 3: Split Setup (Siphon) Best for: Operators who want reward reliability, key isolation, or plan to scale GPU machines independently.
Choose between a single machine setup (full go livepeer) or a split setup that keeps your keystore secure on a separate machine (Siphon).
Consider the Siphon split setup: one secure machine handles your keystore and reward claiming, a separate GPU machine runs go livepeer and processes workloads.

The four paths at a glance Path Who it suits Stake required Ops lift Start here Pool worker GPU owner wanting passive income; no protocol ops No Low Solo orchestrator Single machine, full control, comfortable with ops Yes Medium Split setup (Siphon) Reward safety + key isolation; scale GPU machines independently Yes Medium Enterprise / fleet Data centre, multi node, commercial scale Yes High Solo vs pool vs Siphon: what actually differs It helps to understand what each setup controls before choosing.

snippets: This page is a practical reference: GPU support policy, session limits by card, VRAM tiers by pipeline, and the minimum system stack needed before your node goes live.
It does not require large VRAM – model loading is not involved.
Batch AI inference AI inference requires VRAM.
The table below is a summary – full pipeline by pipeline VRAM figures are in .

Workload quick reference: Workload Min hardware Pricing Selection by gateway Video transcoding Any NVENC capable NVIDIA GPU Wei per pixel; must be below gateway's Stake + price + performance score Batch AI inference 4–24 GB VRAM depending on pipeline Wei per pixel or per request; must be within gateway's capability price range Capability match + price ( ) Real time AI (live video to video) 8 GB+ VRAM Per interval during stream; price advertised per capability Capability match + latency Gateways filter orchestrators by price before routing work.
Understand the four workload categories — transcoding, batch AI, real time AI, and LLM — before you configure your node.

Workload Minimum GPU Recommended GPU Video transcoding only NVIDIA RTX 3060 (8 GB VRAM) RTX 4080 / A4000 Batch AI inference NVIDIA RTX 3090 / A40 (24 GB VRAM) RTX 4090 / A100 Real time AI (Cascade) NVIDIA RTX 3090 (24 GB VRAM) RTX 4090 / A100 LLM inference only NVIDIA RTX 3060 Ti (8 GB VRAM) RTX 3090+ NVIDIA GPUs only.
If your GPU has limited VRAM (under 16 GB), you can start with LLM inference and , which run comfortably at 8–12 GB.

GPU power draw varies significantly: GPU Typical load power draw RTX 3060 ~150–170 W RTX 3090 ~300–350 W RTX 4090 ~350–450 W A100 80 GB ~250–400 W Running an RTX 4090 24/7 at full load costs approximately £30–60/month in electricity at UK residential rates, or $35–70 at US average rates.

Hardware NVIDIA GPU with NVENC/NVDEC support installed and visible via NVIDIA driver version 525+ (Linux) or 527+ (Windows) CUDA 12.0+ installed (or plan to use the Docker image, which bundles CUDA) CPU: 4+ cores minimum, 8+ recommended RAM: 16 GB minimum, 32 GB recommended Storage: 100 GB SSD minimum (NVMe recommended for AI model weights) For AI inference, VRAM is the primary constraint: 8 GB for LLM only, 16 GB for basic batch AI, 24 GB+ for the full pipeline suite.

conflicted_claims.summary: Reward-call profitability guidance should remain explicitly time-sensitive because gas cost, ETH price, and LPT price move. Docs must not present rough thresholds as durable constants.

snippets: This page covers the operational mechanics of how LPT rewards flow, how to call reliably, the economics of when calling reward is profitable, and how ETH fees move from job to wallet.
Calling Reward() — your options Option 1: Automatic (default, recommended) By default, go livepeer automatically calls at the start of each new round.

Option 2: Disable automatic, call manually Use this approach when you are newly activated with very low stake, and want to evaluate whether the gas cost exceeds the LPT value before committing to automatic calling.
Step 1: Disable automatic reward calls Step 2: Estimate whether calling is profitable this round Gather three inputs: 1.
Your orchestrator wallet needs ETH on Arbitrum One for: Reward() calls — one per round, approximately every 22 hours.
Is reward calling profitable for my stake?
If your stake is small, the LPT you'd receive per round may be worth less than the gas cost.

A rough check: open and find your orchestrator's "Estimated Reward This Round." If that LPT value (converted to ETH at the current price) is less than approximately 0.00005 ETH ($0.12 at ETH=$2,500), the gas cost may exceed the reward.

conflicted_claims.summary: A split setup with OrchestratorSiphon isolates the keystore from the GPU machine and keeps reward calling independent of GPU uptime. Docs should keep that claim aligned across setup-path pages.

snippets: If the GPU machine is busy or reboots mid round, you miss that round's LPT inflation reward — and missed rewards compound against you over time.
The split setup solves both.
OrchestratorSiphon runs on a small, secure machine and handles all on chain actions: reward calling, fee withdrawal, governance voting, and service URI updates.
Your GPU machine can restart, be replaced, or be taken offline for maintenance without interrupting reward claims.
Choose between a single machine setup (full go livepeer) or a split setup that keeps your keystore secure on a separate machine (Siphon).
Consider the Siphon split setup: one secure machine handles your keystore and reward claiming, a separate GPU machine runs go livepeer and processes workloads.

The pool controls: on chain registration, staking, pricing, reward calling, payout schedules.

Choose between a single machine setup (full go livepeer) or a split setup that keeps your keystore secure on a separate machine (Siphon).

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: A split setup with OrchestratorSiphon isolates the keystore from the GPU machine and keeps reward calling independent of GPU uptime. Docs should keep that claim aligned across setup-path pages.

values: Stake plays a much smaller role
pages.snippet: Batch AI inference AI jobs route on capability match + price ceiling.

Workload quick reference: Workload Min hardware Pricing Selection by gateway Video transcoding Any NVENC capable NVIDIA GPU Wei per pixel; must be below gateway's Stake + price + performance score Batch AI inference 4–24 GB VRAM depending on pipeline Wei per pixel or per request; must be within gateway's capability price range Capability match + price ( ) Real time AI (live video to video) 8 GB+ VRAM Per interval during stream; price advertised per capability Capability match + latency Gateways filter orchestrators by price before routing work.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: AI jobs are routed primarily by capability match and price ceilings rather than by stake weight. Docs should not collapse AI routing into the same rules as transcoding routing.
